vec![GrantedDatabase {\n            name: DATABASE.to_string(),\n            ops: vec![Op::Read],\n        }],\n    };\n    let token = issuer.issue(&claims).unwrap();\n    let state = make_state();\n    let app = make_app(state);\n\n    // POST = Write, but grant only has Read.\n    let req = Request::builder()\n        .method(\"POST\")\n        .uri(TEST_PATH)\n        .header(\"Authorization\", format!(\"Bearer {token}\"))\n        .body(Body::empty())\n        .unwrap();\n    let (status, json) = send(app, req).await;\n    assert_eq!(status.as_u16(), 403);\n    assert_eq!(error_code(&json), \"op_not_granted\");\n}\n\n// ── valid_jwt_populates_request_extension ─────────────────────────────────────\n\nasync fn identity_handler(\n    Extension(identity): Extension<ResolvedIdentity>,\n) -> impl IntoResponse {\n    axum::Json(serde_json::json!({\"user_id\": identity.user_id.as_str()}))\n}\n\n#[tokio::test]\nasync fn valid_jwt_populates_request_extension() {\n    let issuer = JwtIssuer::new(SECRET.to_vec(), ISSUER_ID.to_string());\n    let claims = valid_claims();\n    let token = issuer.issue(&claims).unwrap();\n\n    let state = make_state();\n    let app = Router::new()\n        .route(TEST_PATH, get(identity_handler))\n        .layer(axum::middleware::from_fn_with_state(\n            state,\n            jwt_verify_layer,\n        ));\n\n    let req = bearer_req(TEST_PATH, &token);\n    let (status, json) = send(app, req).await;\n    assert_eq!(status, StatusCode::OK);\n    assert_eq!(json[\"user_id\"].as_str().unwrap(), USER_ID);\n}\n\n// ── revocation_takes_effect_within_one_second ─────────────────────────────────\n\n#[tokio::test]\nasync fn revocation_takes_effect_within_one_second() {\n    let issuer = JwtIssuer::new(SECRET.to_vec(), ISSUER_ID.to_string());\n    let claims = valid_claims();\n    let jti_uuid: Uuid = claims.jti.parse().unwrap();\n    let token = issuer.issue(&claims).unwrap();\n\n    let state = make_state();\n    let app_before = make_app(state.clone());\n    let app_after = make_app(state.clone());\n\n    // First request must succeed.\n    let (status, _) = send(app_before, bearer_req(TEST_PATH, &token)).await;\n    assert_eq!(status, StatusCode::OK, \"before revocation should be 200\");\n\n    // Directly invalidate the cache — no sleep needed.\n    state.revocation_cache.insert(jti_uuid);\n\n    // Second request must fail with credential_revoked.\n    let (status, json) = send(app_after, bearer_req(TEST_PATH, &token)).await;\n    assert_eq!(status, StatusCode::UNAUTHORIZED);\n    assert_eq!(error_code(&json), \"credential_revoked\");\n}\n\n// ── clock_skew_30s_accepted_31s_rejected token = issuer.issue(&claims).unwrap();\n    let state = make_state();\n    let (status, json) = send(make_app(state), bearer_req(TEST_PATH, &token)).await;\n    assert_eq!(status, let token = issuer.issue(&claims).unwrap();\n    let state = make_state();\n    let (status, _) = send(make_app(state), bearer_req(TEST_PATH, &token)).await;\n    assert_eq!(status, StatusCode::OK);\n}\n\n#[tokio::test]\nasync fn clock_skew_nbf_31s_future_rejected() {\n    let issuer = JwtIssuer::new(SECRET.to_vec(), ISSUER_ID.to_string());\n    let now = now_secs();\n    let mut claims = valid_claims();\n    claims.nbf = now + 31; // 31 s from now — beyond the 30s skew window\n    let token = issuer.issue(&claims).unwrap();\n    let state = make_state();\n    let (status, json) = send(make_app(state), bearer_req(TEST_PATH, &token)).await;\n    assert_eq!(status, StatusCode::UNAUTHORIZED);\n    assert_eq!(error_code(&json), \"credential_not_yet_valid\");\n}\n\n// ── ops_matrix ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n#[tokio::test]\nasync fn ops_matrix() {\n    use http::Method;\n\n    struct Case {\n        method: Method,\n        ops: Vec<Op>,\n        expected_status: u16,\n    }\n    let cases = vec![\n        // GET (Read) against various grant sets\n        Case { method: Method::GET, ops: vec![Op::Read], expected_status: 200 },\n        Case { method: Method::GET, ops: vec![Op::Write], expected_status: 403 },\n        Case { method: Method::GET, ops: vec![Op::Read, Op::Write], expected_status: 200 },\n        // Write methods (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) against various grant sets\n        Case { method: Method::POST, ops: vec![Op::Write], expected_status: 200 },\n        Case { method: Method::POST, ops: vec![Op::Read], expected_status: 403 },\n        Case { method: Method::POST, ops: vec![Op::Read, Op::Write], expected_status: 200 },\n        Case { method: Method::PUT, ops: vec![Op::Write], expected_status: 200 },\n        Case { method: Method::PUT, ops: vec![Op::Read], expected_status: 403 },\n        Case { method: Method::PATCH, ops: vec![Op::Write], expected_status: 200 },\n        Case { method: Method::PATCH, ops: vec![Op::Read], expected_status: 403 },\n        Case { method: Method::DELETE, ops: vec![Op::Write], expected_status: 200 },\n        Case { method: Method::DELETE, ops: vec![Op::Read], expected_status: 403 },\n    ];\n\n    // Use a route that accepts all HTTP methods.\n    let handler = || async { StatusCode::OK };\n\n    for case in cases {\n        let issuer_inner = JwtIssuer::new(SECRET.to_vec(), ISSUER_ID.to_string());\n        let mut claims = valid_claims();\n        claims.grants = Grants {\n            databases: vec![GrantedDatabase {\n                name: DATABASE.to_string(),\n                ops: case.ops,\n            }],\n        };\n        let token = issuer_inner.issue(&claims).unwrap();\n\n        let state = make_state();\n        // Router that handles any method on TEST_PATH.\n        let app = Router::new()\n            .route(\n                TEST_PATH,\n                axum::routing::any(handler),\n            )\n            .layer(axum::middleware::from_fn_with_state(\n                state,\n                jwt_verify_layer,\n            ));\n\n        let req = Request::builder()\n            .method(case.method.clone())\n            .uri(TEST_PATH)\n            .header(\"Authorization\", format!(\"Bearer {token}\"))\n            .body(Body::empty())\n            .unwrap();\n        let resp = app.oneshot(req).await.unwrap();\n        assert_eq!(\n            resp.status().as_u16(),\n            case.expected_status,\n            \"method={} expected {}\",\n            case.method,\n            case.expected_status\n        );\n    }\n}\n\n// ── error_code / status_code consistency ──────────────────────────────────────\n\n#[test]\nfn auth_error_status_and_code_coverage() {\n    // Verify every variant maps to the correct status and code string.\n    let cases: &[(AuthError, u16, &str)] = &[\n        (AuthError::Unauthenticated, 401, \"unauthenticated\"),\n        (AuthError::CredentialMalformed, 401, \"credential_malformed\"),\n        (AuthError::CredentialInvalid, 401, \"credential_invalid\"),\n        (AuthError::CredentialExpired, 401, \"credential_expired\"),\n        (AuthError::CredentialNotYetValid, 401, \"credential_not_yet_valid\"),\n        (AuthError::CredentialRevoked, 401, \"credential_revoked\"),\n        (AuthError::CredentialForeignIssuer, 401, \"credential_foreign_issuer\"),\n        (AuthError::CredentialWrongTenant, 403, \"credential_wrong_tenant\"),\n        (AuthError::UserSuspended, 401, \"user_suspended\"),\n        (AuthError::NotATenantMember, 403, \"not_a_tenant_member\"),\n        (AuthError::DatabaseNotGranted, 403, \"database_not_granted\"),\n        (AuthError::OpNotGranted, 403, \"op_not_granted\"),\n        (AuthError::GrantsExceedIssuerRole, 401, \"grants_exceed_issuer_role\"),\n        (AuthError::GrantsMalformed, 401, \"grants_malformed\"),\n    ];\n    for (err, expected_status, expected_code) in cases {\n        assert_eq!(\n            err.status_code(),\n            *expected_status,\n            \"status mismatch for {:?}\",\n            err\n        );\n        assert_eq!(\n            err.error_code(),\n            *expected_code,\n            \"code mismatch for {:?}\",\n            err\n        );\n    }\n}\n\n// ── revocation via storage fallback ───────────────────────────────────────────\n\n#[tokio::test]\nasync fn revocation_via_storage_fallback() {\n    let issuer = JwtIssuer::new(SECRET.to_vec(), ISSUER_ID.to_string());\n    let claims = storage = make_storage();\n    storage.revoke_jti(jti_uuid);\n\n    let state = Arc::new(AuthPipelineState {\n        issuer: Arc::new(JwtIssuer::new(SECRET.to_vec(), ISSUER_ID.to_string())),\n        revocation_cache: Arc::new(InMemoryRevocationCache::new()),\n        storage: Arc::new(Mutex::new(\n            Box::new(storage) as Box<dyn axon_storage::StorageAdapter + Send + Sync>\n        )),\n    });\n    let app = make_app(state);\n    let (status, json) = send(app, bearer_req(TEST_PATH, &token)).await;\n    assert_eq!(status, StatusCode::UNAUTHORIZED);\n    assert_eq!(error_code(&json), {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Base directory for this skill: /home/erik/.claude/skills/helix-commit\n\n# Commit: HELIX-Compliant Git Commit\n\nCreate a well-formatted commit with issue traceability, run the project's\nbuild gate, push, and optionally update the tracker.\n\n## When to Use\n\n- After implementing a tracked issue\n- When you need a properly formatted commit message\n- When codex or another agent needs commit support\n\n## What It Does\n\n1. **Inspect** staged changes (`git diff --cached --stat`)\n2. **Format** commit message:\n   - First line: `<issue-id> <concise summary>` (under 72 chars)\n   - Body: what changed and why, governing artifact refs\n   - Trailer: verification commands run\n3. **Pre-push gate**: run the project's build check before pushing\n   - `lefthook run pre-commit` if available\n   - `cargo check --workspace` for Rust projects\n   - `npm test` for Node.js projects\n4. **Commit and push**: `git commit`, `git pull --rebase`, `git push`\n5. **Tracker update** (if issue-id provided): close the issue\n\n## Commit Message Format\n\n```\nhx-abc123 Short summary of the change\n\nLonger description of what changed and why. Reference governing\nartifacts when relevant (SD-001, TP-002, etc.).\n\nVerification: cargo test -p changed-crate, cargo clippy\n```\n\n## Examples\n\n```bash\nhelix commit hx-abc123      # commit, gate, push, close issue\nhelix commit                 # commit and gate without tracker update\n```\n"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"80f0eb9d-4064-4d06-8560-e247ce1170f2","uuid":"389e7b5b-4f0d-4836-a072-b1bc977baa31","timestamp":"2026-04-15T01:49:25.805Z","isSynthetic":true}

.ddx/attachments/ddx-88e91fdb/events.jsonl:3:{"actor":"ddx","body":"Bead: ddx-88e91fdb — Add chat interface to project home page

Disposition: no commit this pass; recommend decomposing into 4–5 smaller beads.

(a) What is done in this pass
- Read the bead description and 12 acceptance criteria.
- Mapped the existing surface area:
  - GraphQL schema at cli/internal/server/graphql/schema.graphql with gqlgen-generated resolvers; subscription pattern established in resolver_sub_worker.go / resolver_sub_bead.go / resolver_sub_exec.go (buffered channel + ctx-cancel goroutine).
  - Frontend GraphQL via graphql-request (queries) + graphql-ws (subscriptions) — see cli/internal/server/frontend/src/lib/gql/{client.ts,subscriptions.ts}. No Houdini.
  - Project home page at cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/+page.svelte (queue summary + 3 action cards). This is the host surface for the chat pane.
  - Sessions index at cli/internal/agent/session_index.go, sharded JSONL under .ddx/sessions/ (monthly). agentSessions GraphQL query already exists. This is the linkage target for AC5.
  - Agent execution entry at cli/internal/agent/service_run.go (RunWithConfigViaService → executeOnService → records a session-index row). Streaming today is per-harness; the execute-loop streams events via worker progress, not per-prompt chunks.
  - Playwright e2e pattern in cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/**/*.e2e.ts uses page.route('/graphql') interception with fixture data; no live backend.

(b) What is blocking a one-pass implementation
The bead bundles a feature that fans out across at least 5 independently-reviewable surfaces:

  1. Storage layer (.ddx/chat/<thread-id>/{manifest.json,messages.jsonl}) — new Go package with tests, append-only invariants, project/operator scoping.
  2. GraphQL schema + resolvers — chatThreads/chatThread/chatMessages queries, chatThreadCreate/chatSend mutations, chatStream subscription. Requires schema.graphql edits, gqlgen regeneration of generated.go, models.go updates, four new resolver files, integration tests.
  3. service.Execute integration — chatSend must dispatch a session and stream chunks + tool calls back over the chatStream subscription. Today service.Execute is request/response with a non-streaming Result; per-chunk streaming over the subscription bus is new infrastructure. Either: (i) add a streaming variant of executeOnService, or (ii) tail the session's evidence stream and re-emit. Both have non-trivial design choices that belong in the design note before code lands.
  4. Frontend — chat pane component, thread list, harness/profile picker, streaming render, tool-call inline rendering, Stop button (AC7), cumulative cost header (AC5/AC8). Real-time updates via subscribeChatMessages helper. Roughly parallel in size to the workers detail page.
  5. Auth posture middleware (AC9) — even as a "single-operator localhost / tsnet for v1" stance, the design note must commit to a posture and a route-gate stub must exist so future auth is schema-local. The bead description flags this as load-bearing for the "remote ops" framing.
  6. Design note in docs/helix/02-design/ answering the six subsidiary questions (persistence scope, harness selection, cost accountability, tool-execution scope, concurrency, auth). The bead's AC1 makes this a hard prerequisite — the implementation PR is supposed to *link* to a landed design note. The note must precede or accompany the implementation; it cannot be a postscript.
  7. Playwright e2e (AC10) — five-step scenario including two browser contexts on the same thread, cancel mid-flight, streamed chunk ordering. Mocking the GraphQL subscription side of this is non-trivial; the existing e2e suite mocks queries via page.route but does not exercise graphql-ws subscriptions.

A single execute-bead commit covering all of the above would either (i) be partial across most of those surfaces and red, or (ii) take many hours of focused work and produce a sprawling diff that the review gate cannot meaningfully verify in one pass. Both outcomes are worse than a clean decomposition.

Additional concrete unknowns the design note must resolve before code can land cleanly:
- Is the chatStream subscription chunk-level (assistant text deltas) or event-level (one event per tool call + one final assistant message)? This determines whether service.Execute needs a new streaming surface or can be wrapped post-hoc.
- For multi-operator on the same thread (AC6), is the second operator a read-only observer or an equal participant who can also send? The bead defaults to "two browsers on the same thread see each other within 2s" which implies multi-participant — but the cost-accountability and session-attribution model needs a per-message author identity that today's auth posture (single-operator) does not surface.
- Does chatSend run in the project worktree or a per-thread worktree? Bead suggests "same as ddx agent run" as the default, but if two threads chatSend concurrently they will race the worktree. This argues for per-thread worktree, which is a real cost (disk, fs setup) and belongs in the design note.

(c) What a follow-up attempt would need

Recommend splitting this bead into the following sub-beads, executed in order:

  Sub-bead 1: Design note (docs/helix/02-design/chat-interface.md)
    - Picks Option A vs B vs C vs D and locks it.
    - Answers the six subsidiary questions.
    - Specifies the message/thread schema (manifest.json shape, messages.jsonl entry shape).
    - Specifies whether chatStream is chunk-level or event-level.
    - Specifies worktree scoping per thread.
    - Specifies auth posture for v1 and the gap to broader deployment.
    Size: 1 pass, design only. No code.

  Sub-bead 2: Chat thread storage package (cli/internal/chat/)
    - New Go package: ChatThread, ChatMessage, AppendMessage, LoadThread, ListThreads.
    - Append-only messages.jsonl invariants, atomic manifest writes.
    - Unit tests including concurrent append safety.
    Size: 1 pass.

  Sub-bead 3: GraphQL surface (schema + resolvers + integration tests)
    - schema.graphql edits, gqlgen regen, four resolver files, integration tests.
    - chatSend dispatches via service.Execute and writes the assistant turn as a single message once the session completes (event-level streaming, simplest first cut). Chunk-level streaming is a follow-up.
    - chatStream subscription emits message-created events.
    - Session linkage via SessionIndexEntry.id reference on each assistant message.
    Size: 1 pass.

  Sub-bead 4: Frontend chat pane
    - New ChatPane.svelte component, thread list, message list, input box, harness/profile picker.
    - Mounted on project home page below the actions section.
    - subscribeChatMessages helper in src/lib/gql/subscriptions.ts.
    - Cost rollup in thread header. Stop button calls workerStop on the in-flight session's worker.
    Size: 1 pass.

  Sub-bead 5: Playwright e2e + auth posture middleware
    - Two-context test, cancel test, streaming chunk test (with mocked graphql-ws server).
    - Auth middleware gate stub on chat routes.
    Size: 1 pass.

The bead's AC11 (depends-on ddx-2513f08c, ddx-2ceb02fa) was not verified in this pass. Sub-bead 1 should re-verify those deps before locking the design.

Recommendation: re-queue this bead as a tracking parent and create the five sub-beads above. The current bead description is unusually thorough and most of its content can be lifted verbatim into the design-note sub-bead's body.

.ddx/executions/20260506T080416-b75944f2/manifest.json:12:    "description": "PROBLEM
  The drain loop tracks attempted and hookFailed beads via ad-hoc in-memory maps rather than a structured Guard contract, making the loop body untestable in isolation and the skip policy implicit.

ROOT CAUSE
  cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go:403-413 declares attempted := make(map[string]struct{}) and hookFailed := make(map[string]struct{}). Lines 534-544 manually clear both maps between sleep cycles. Lines 591-601 implement the two-strikes pre-claim hook policy by mutating these maps inline. nextCandidate (line 1216) accepts attempted as a parameter and checks membership at line 1223. No Guard interface exists; the pre-claim logic and the complexity gate each embed their skip logic directly in the loop body.

PROPOSED FIX
  - Define Guard interface in cli/internal/agent/work/guard.go: Allow(ctx context.Context, beadID string) (bool, reason string).
  - Implement two concrete Guards: PreClaimGuard (wraps runtime.PreClaimHook two-strikes logic) and ComplexityGuard (wraps w.ComplexityGate nil-check).
  - Delete attempted and hookFailed maps from execute_bead_loop.go:403-413. Replace nextCandidate's attempted parameter with drain.result.Results slice (already populated per-iteration): filter beads whose ID appears in Results.
  - Pre-claim hook denial: call w.Store.SetExecutionCooldown(candidate.ID, 30s, ...) instead of recording in hookFailed.
  - Add TestGuard_PreClaim_TwoStrikesSkips and TestGuard_Complexity_NilGateWarnsOnce to a new cli/internal/agent/work/guard_test.go.

NON-SCOPE
  - Heartbeat extraction (C11, ddx-0b8780db).
  - File renames (C13, ddx-387a0178).
  - Any change to SetExecutionCooldown duration policy (controlled by caller constants).

INTERSECTIONS
- P1: guard denials should skip the candidate rather than wedge the queue.
- P2: each guard owns only its own skip policy.
- P4: a denied bead stays isolated from other beads.",

.ddx/executions/20260506T030402-e17ce643/prompt.md:8:PROBLEM
  The drain loop tracks attempted and hookFailed beads via ad-hoc in-memory maps rather than a structured Guard contract, making the loop body untestable in isolation and the skip policy implicit.

ROOT CAUSE
  cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go:403-413 declares attempted := make(map[string]struct{}) and hookFailed := make(map[string]struct{}). Lines 534-544 manually clear both maps between sleep cycles. Lines 591-601 implement the two-strikes pre-claim hook policy by mutating these maps inline. nextCandidate (line 1216) accepts attempted as a parameter and checks membership at line 1223. No Guard interface exists; the pre-claim logic and the complexity gate each embed their skip logic directly in the loop body.

PROPOSED FIX
  - Define Guard interface in cli/internal/agent/work/guard.go: Allow(ctx context.Context, beadID string) (bool, reason string).
  - Implement two concrete Guards: PreClaimGuard (wraps runtime.PreClaimHook two-strikes logic) and ComplexityGuard (wraps w.ComplexityGate nil-check).
  - Delete attempted and hookFailed maps from execute_bead_loop.go:403-413. Replace nextCandidate's attempted parameter with drain.result.Results slice (already populated per-iteration): filter beads whose ID appears in Results.
  - Pre-claim hook denial: call w.Store.SetExecutionCooldown(candidate.ID, 30s, ...) instead of recording in hookFailed.
  - Add TestGuard_PreClaim_TwoStrikesSkips and TestGuard_Complexity_NilGateWarnsOnce to a new cli/internal/agent/work/guard_test.go.

NON-SCOPE
  - Heartbeat extraction (C11, ddx-0b8780db).
  - File renames (C13, ddx-387a0178).
  - Any change to SetExecutionCooldown duration policy (controlled by caller constants).

INTERSECTIONS
- P1: guard denials should skip the candidate rather than wedge the queue.
- P2: each guard owns only its own skip policy.
- P4: a denied bead stays isolated from other beads.

Conventional commit ending [<this-bead-id>].","closing_commit_sha":"baf5f748920779c3379712225341fe58ec9be46c","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:41.651384066Z","description":"The ddx try / ddx work execution machinery has 9 fail-prone layers (routing pre-flight, cooldown classification, claim CAS, worktree spawn, subprocess lifecycle, outcome adjudication, retry policy, land coordination, review). Today most layers fail-CLOSED — when a layer's specific check rejects, the layer wedges the entire pipeline. The auto-routing rejection bug fixed at commit 3b4f5d58 demonstrated this: one preflight check went strict and the queue locked up for hours.

The 7 principles below capture what each layer should do instead. They are documentation, not code — referenced by every refactor child's AC and by future bead-authoring guidance.

PRINCIPLE STATEMENTS

P1: FAIL-OPEN at every machinery layer. When a layer's specific check rejects a candidate, the layer skips itself and emits a structured event — it does not wedge the pipeline. The auto-routing fix (workers.go:803, commit 3b4f5d58) is the canonical example: preflight is now advisory when no operator pin exists.

P2: SINGLE RESPONSIBILITY per layer. Each layer rejects only on conditions it owns. Routing pre-flight does NOT decide provider availability (fizeau owns); cooldown does NOT decide eligibility (picker owns); etc. Cross-layer concerns are the smell.

P3: OBSERVABLE DEGRADATION. Every fail-open emits a structured event surfaced in the workers panel. Operators see "preflight skipped (no operator pin)" instead of silent acceptance OR endless retry loop.

P4: BOUNDED BLAST RADIUS. A failure on bead X must not affect bead Y. Stay-alive fix at commit 41cb762e established this for preflight rejections (per-bead continue, not loop exit). Extend to all layers.

P5: OPERATOR-VISIBLE STATE. Worker reports current state (idle, claiming, executing, reviewing, blocked-on-X) at all times. No '8 hours running, 0 attempts' mystery state. ADR-022 rev 5 §Probe + freshness state model defines the worker side; UI workers panel surfaces it.

P6: AUTO-RETRY ONLY FOR TRANSIENT CLASSES. Cooldown fires ONLY when the model genuinely couldn't make progress (clean no-changes with rationale). Disrupted, preflight-rejected, network-error, claim-race → no cooldown, return to ready. Existing code: shouldSuppressNoProgress at execute_bead_loop.go:1545 already respects Disrupted (commit 47d8054e).

P7: BEAD = PROMPT. A bead's description + AC must be sufficient context for a competent sub-agent to execute it without hand-curation. Investigation done, file:line citations included, concrete test names specified, explicit non-scope marked. If a sub-agent succeeds where the bead's auto-prompt failed, the BEAD failed (not the executor). Bead-authoring template enforces this; bead-quality audit (forthcoming bead) retrofits existing beads.

NOT IN SCOPE
- Code changes (this bead is doc-only)
- Per-layer reliability bead-quality fixes (separate refactor children)

INTERSECTIONS
- Each refactor child (ddx-c8f79963 C5, ddx-06eb05d8 C7, ddx-9228a484 C9, ddx-848069a3 C8, ddx-b669bb9f C6, ddx-c670ef0a C12) gets an AC line citing the principle(s) it enforces
- Bead-quality audit applies P7 to ~20 existing open beads

EVIDENCE
- ddx-f339c399 documents the 2026-05-04 ddx try ddx-29058e2a --harness=codex no_changes outcome and supports P7 / bead-quality.","events_attachment":"ddx-06b77652/events.jsonl","execute-loop-last-detail":"exit status 1","execute-loop-last-status":"execution_failed","execute-loop-no-changes-count":1,"execute-loop-retry-after":"2026-05-05T04:20:59Z","id":"ddx-06b77652","issue_type":"task","labels":["phase:2","area:agent","area:server","kind:design","reliability","bead-quality"],"notes":"Evidence cross-link: ddx-f339c399 documents the 2026-05-04 ddx try ddx-29058e2a --harness=codex no_changes outcome and serves as evidence for P7 / bead-quality.","parent":"ddx-e34994e2","priority":2,"session_id":"eb-249cd7a8","status":"closed","title":"ADR-style: 7 reliability principles for ddx try / ddx work / sub-agent execution","updated_at":"2026-05-05T14:19:39.552645934Z"}

  The heartbeat goroutine in the drain loop is inlined, coupling ticker lifecycle management with candidate execution and blocking unit tests from verifying heartbeat behavior without a real ticker.

ROOT CAUSE
  cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go:732-753 inlines a goroutine + sync.WaitGroup + time.NewTicker(heartbeatInterval) directly in the loop body. hbCtx and hbWG are declared locally; the goroutine calls w.Store.Heartbeat(beadID) on every tick. There is no injection seam, so tests cannot stub the ticker to verify heartbeat cadence or cancellation without real wall-clock delays.

PROPOSED FIX
  - Extract to withHeartbeat(ctx context.Context, beadID string, interval time.Duration, store interface{ Heartbeat(string) error }, fn func() (ExecuteBeadReport, error)) error in a new file cli/internal/agent/work/heartbeat.go.
  - withHeartbeat starts the internal goroutine + WaitGroup, calls fn(), cancels the ticker goroutine on fn return, and returns fn's result.
  - Drain loop body (execute_bead_loop.go:732) replaces the inline goroutine block with a single withHeartbeat call.
  - Add TestWithHeartbeat_TickerFiredBeforeReturn and TestWithHeartbeat_CancelPropagates to cli/internal/agent/work/heartbeat_test.go using a stub ticker channel injection.

NON-SCOPE
  - File renames (C13, ddx-387a0178).
  - Changes to heartbeatInterval policy or the Store.Heartbeat call itself.
  - Any behavior change to the drain loop; this is a pure extraction.

INTERSECTIONS
- P2: heartbeat timing and lifecycle belong in one helper, not inline with candidate execution.
- P4: one bead heartbeat bug must not leak into the rest of the drain loop.
- P5: extracted heartbeat behavior supports always-visible worker state.","id":"ddx-0b8780db","issue_type":"task","labels":["phase:2","refactor","kind:refactor"],"parent":"ddx-5cb6e6cd","priority":2,"status":"open","title":"C11: heartbeat extracted to withHeartbeat helper","updated_at":"2026-05-06T05:05:24.787964532Z"}

DEPS (gate execution; bead stays open until these close): refactor children C4-C13 (ddx-358f2457, ddx-c8f79963, ddx-b669bb9f, ddx-06eb05d8, ddx-848069a3, ddx-9228a484, ddx-0b8780db, ddx-c670ef0a, ddx-387a0178).","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:41.880567652Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-358f2457","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-c8f79963","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-b669bb9f","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-06eb05d8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-848069a3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-9228a484","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-0b8780db","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-c670ef0a","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-387a0178","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"}],"description":"Hand-curated sub-agent prompts have been outperforming ddx work / ddx try in this session. Two competing hypotheses for why:
H1: ddx execution machinery (the 9 fail-prone layers) is unreliable — failure is in the workers/server code.
H2: Bead authoring quality is insufficient — beads punt investigation; hand-curation papers over the gap.

Both are plausible. Determining the relative contribution requires a 3-path study, NOT the original 2-path proposal.

THREE PATHS

Path A: ddx try / ddx work with the bead's auto-prompt verbatim (current production path).
Path B: Sub-agent dispatched with the bead's auto-prompt verbatim (NOT hand-curated). Same prompt as A; different executor. Isolates ddx machinery from prompt quality.
Path C: Sub-agent dispatched with operator hand-curated prompt (file:line cites, named tests, explicit non-scope, etc.). Establishes the ceiling.

INTERPRETATION
- A vs B differ → ddx machinery is the failure (file follow-ups against the 9 layers).
- B vs C differ → bead authoring quality is the failure (already covered by bead-quality-audit + retrofit).
- A == B << C → both are real, with bead quality being the dominant gap.

METHOD
Pick 5 representative CLOSED beads as fixtures: one trivial fix, one refactor child, one test addition, one multi-file feature, one documentation update. For each fixture, run all 3 paths on a branch from the bead's base-rev (parent of the original work commit). Throwaway commits — the original work is already in main. We're measuring path properties, not producing mergeable code.

MEASUREMENTS
Per path per bead:
- Time-to-first-claim (A) / time-to-first-output (B,C)
- Time-to-merge-eligible-commit (whether or not we merge)
- Attempts (A) / iterations (B,C)
- Status outcomes: success / no_changes / preserved / blocked / errored
- Cost: tokens, USD
- Manual interventions
- Failure mode classified per the 9-layer machinery list

OUTPUT
.ddx/executions/<run-id>/ddx-vs-subagent-comparison-2026-05-03.md with:
- Per-bead per-path table
- Aggregate stats (median + p99)
- Failure-mode distribution
- Hypothesis verdict (H1 vs H2 vs both)
- Recommendations for which path is appropriate for which bead-shape

TIMING
This study runs AFTER the executeloop refactor (C0-C13) lands so the ddx machinery is its post-refactor shape. Running pre-refactor measures behavior we're about to change. RELIABILITY-PRINCIPLES + bead-quality-audit + retrofit + refactor must all complete first. This bead is filed now and EXECUTION DEFERRED.

NOT IN SCOPE
- Fixing whatever the study reveals (separate bug beads filed from the study's findings)
- Running against pre-refactor ddx machinery (data we won't act on)
- Comparing different sub-agent harnesses (different concern)

EVIDENCE
- ddx-f339c399 documents the 2026-05-04 ddx try ddx-29058e2a --harness=codex no_changes outcome and is partial Path B-vs-C data favoring the bead-quality hypothesis.","id":"ddx-31f745cd","issue_type":"task","labels":["phase:2","area:agent","area:tooling","kind:study","reliability","bead-quality"],"notes":"Partial Path B-vs-C evidence: ddx-f339c399 documents the 2026-05-04 ddx try ddx-29058e2a --harness=codex no_changes outcome, supporting the bead-quality hypothesis side of the study.","parent":"ddx-e34994e2","priority":2,"status":"open","title":"study: 3-path execution comparison (ddx work vs sub-agent + verbatim bead vs sub-agent + hand-curated)","updated_at":"2026-05-05T03:08:30.860761453Z"}

  The conflict-recovery state machine (auto-merge + focused-resolve + close-or-park) is partially extracted but the description cites only the pre-extraction loop lines, leaving the executor without a clear picture of the current API boundary.

ROOT CAUSE
  cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_conflict_recovery.go:118 defines RunConflictRecovery(ctx, ConflictRecoveryInput) ConflictRecoveryOutput — the C4 extraction already landed. The bead description still refers to the old inline branch. The caller site is cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go:847-878 (loop branches on ShouldAttemptConflictRecovery, then calls RunConflictRecovery). The try.Attempt API (cli/internal/agent/try/attempt.go:67, func Attempt(ctx, store, beadID, AttemptOpts) (Outcome, error)) does not yet call RunConflictRecovery; conflict recovery remains loop-owned.

PROPOSED FIX
  - Migrate RunConflictRecovery invocation from execute_bead_loop.go:847-878 into try.Attempt (cli/internal/agent/try/attempt.go) so Attempt's return Outcome already encodes conflict-recovery disposition.
  - Add try/conflict_recovery.go: thin adapter from ConflictRecoveryOutput to Outcome (Disposition=Merged → OutcomeSuccess, Park/NeedsHuman → OutcomePark).
  - Loop caller (execute_bead_loop.go:847) delegates to try.Attempt instead of calling RunConflictRecovery directly; removes the inline branch.
  - Add TestAttempt_ConflictRecovered_ReturnsMergedOutcome and TestAttempt_ConflictUnresolvable_ReturnsPark to cli/internal/agent/try/attempt_test.go.

NON-SCOPE
  - Deleting the attempted/hookFailed maps (C7, ddx-06eb05d8).
  - Heartbeat extraction (C11, ddx-0b8780db).
  - File renames (C13, ddx-387a0178).

INTERSECTIONS
- P1: fail-open at the layer boundary when conflict recovery is not applicable or cannot complete cleanly.
- P2: conflict recovery owns only its own conditions; the loop should delegate rather than infer recovery policy.
- P3: the worker panel should surface conflict-recovery transitions and any degraded path.

  The execute-bead subsystem files use execute_bead_* naming that conflates the orchestrator, the attempt, the review, and the loop under one prefix, making package boundaries and import graphs ambiguous. The refactor proposal was stored at /tmp/ and is unreadable post-session.

ROOT CAUSE
  cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go (drain loop), cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go (attempt), cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_orchestrator.go (land coordinator), cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go (landing), cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go (review), cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review_triage.go (review triage), cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_status.go (disposition), and cli/internal/agent/executions_mirror.go (mirror) all share a flat package with unrelated concerns. The ddx try CLI command named in FEAT-010 does not exist (cli/cmd/try.go absent).

PROPOSED FIX
  Rename per the following table (no behavior change):
    cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go             -> cli/internal/agent/try/attempt.go (already exists; merge or verify)
    cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_orchestrator.go -> cli/internal/agent/try/land.go
    cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go         -> cli/internal/agent/try/coordinator.go
    cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go       -> cli/internal/agent/try/review.go
    cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review_triage.go -> cli/internal/agent/try/review_triage.go
    cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_status.go       -> cli/internal/agent/try/disposition.go
    cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go         -> cli/internal/agent/work/drain.go
    cli/internal/agent/executions_mirror.go         -> cli/internal/agent/try/mirror.go

  Rename identifiers: ExecuteBeadWorker -> work.Worker, ExecuteBeadWorker.Run -> work.Worker.Drain, ExecuteBead -> try.Attempt (FEAT-006 + FEAT-010).

  Add cli/cmd/try.go with cobra command "ddx try <bead-id>" invoking try.Attempt directly (FEAT-010 surface).

  Update FEAT-006 and FEAT-010 spec docs to reflect new package paths. Keep ExecuteBead* shims as typedefs; deletion is C14.

NON-SCOPE
  - Behavior changes to the drain loop or attempt logic.
  - Deletion of ExecuteBead* typedefs (C14).
  - ddx-dc157075 file-path reference migration if that bead has not yet landed.

INTERSECTIONS
- P2: file and identifier names should reflect the subsystem boundary and keep responsibilities separated.
- P5: ddx try as a first-class CLI surface makes the worker/attempt state easier for operators to see.
- P7: the refactor aligns names and prompt surface with the bead-as-prompt contract.","id":"ddx-387a0178","issue_type":"task","labels":["phase:2","refactor","kind:refactor"],"parent":"ddx-5cb6e6cd","priority":1,"status":"open","title":"C13: file/identifier rename per refactor proposal §3 + ADD ddx try CLI command + handle dc157075 path migration","updated_at":"2026-05-05T07:43:38.398872469Z"}

ExecuteBeadWorker.Run() is a 700-line function with 24 returns: 4 legitimate, 1 the poll-interval-zero exit, 1 the routing-preflight-kills-worker exit, and 18 store-error escalations from the same handleOutcomeStoreError pattern repeated 14 times. The user undercounted the smell by ~5×. The implicit state machine in the loop is real and coherent (Pick → Gate → Claim → Execute → Classify → Reviewing → Terminal) but written as conditional soup instead of explicit state.

## Three-package split aligned to FEAT-010

  cli/internal/agent/run/   — Layer 1: invocation atom (existing dispatch + runner)
  cli/internal/agent/try/   — Layer 2: bead attempt (worktree, prompt, single Execute, Land, conflict-recovery, post-merge review, triage)
  cli/internal/agent/work/  — Layer 3: queue drain (pick, claim, heartbeat, drain-loop control, stop conditions)

try.Attempt(beadID) returns sealed Outcome { Disposition (enum), Cooldown, ParkReason, RecordEvents }. Loop becomes ~30 lines: pick → guard → claim → attemptWithHeartbeat → applyOutcome. Single commitOutcome helper deletes the 18 store-error returns.

## Ordering: refactor-LAST per user decision

In-flight Group A beads (c6e3db02 rate-limit, b24e9630 no_changes, cfedee8e ladder wire, a8718bec cost-cap reviewer) land first against current code. dc157075 tactical worker-stay-alive lands first as operational bridge. THIS refactor lands last and absorbs all intervening behavior into the new architecture. Per-child ACs explicitly call out which in-flight bead's behavior each child must preserve.

## Naming

Package = try (not try_bead — Go idiom). Function = try.Attempt(beadID). Loop = work.Worker.Drain. CLI surface (ddx run / try / work) already correct; C13 also ADDS ddx try as a new CLI command (FEAT-010 named it but it doesn't exist yet).

## Children

  C0  Pre-refactor JSONL fixture baseline for event-shape regression detection
  C1  try.Outcome sealed sum type + Disposition enum + ParkReason vocabulary enumeration
  C2  try.Attempt function shell (wraps existing executor; no behavior change)
  C3  Move review verdict handling into try.Attempt (180 lines from loop body)
  C4  Move conflict recovery into try.Attempt
  C5  Move no_changes adjudication, decomposition, push-failed, push-conflict (absorbs ddx-b24e9630, ddx-c6e3db02 behavior)
  C6  Single commitOutcome helper; deletes 18 store-error exit paths + handleOutcomeStoreError
  C7  Uniform Guard contract; delete attempted/hookFailed maps; pick() derives from drain.result
  C8  Routing preflight moves to Drain startup (runs once, not per iteration)
  C9  First-class StopCondition enum + cost-cap relocation (absorbs ddx-cfedee8e ladder + ddx-a8718bec reviewer cost + ddx-dc157075 tactical work-stay-alive). Includes downstream-consumer audit for execution_failed:max-cost grep before relocation. Includes 30s default for ddx work --poll-interval.
  C10 ddx work stay-alive default + transient idle substate (subsumes ddx-dc157075 fully)
  C11 Heartbeat extracted to withHeartbeat helper
  C12 Single phase-event emission (replaces 4 sites)
  C13 File and identifier rename per §3 of proposal + ADD ddx try CLI command + handle dc157075 file-path migration if it landed first
  C14 Delete ExecuteBead* shims; verify all callers ported off (grep audit)","id":"ddx-5cb6e6cd","issue_type":"epic","labels":["phase:2","refactor","area:agent"],"parent":"ddx-b18d9ce6","priority":1,"status":"open","title":"REFACTOR: execute-bead surface → try / work packages aligned to FEAT-010","updated_at":"2026-05-02T20:00:48.783782958Z"}
.ddx/beads.jsonl:644:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As an operator using a browser — on a machine without a terminal, or a phone, or a laptop away from my dev box — I can open my project's home page, type a prompt into a chat pane, and see the DDx agent work on it in real time. Tool calls stream as they happen, costs accrue visibly, and the conversation persists so I can come back to it.

**Acceptance criteria (written for Option A; rewrite if design note chooses B/C/D):**

1. **Design note landed.** One-pager in `docs/helix/02-design/` records the chosen option, the answers to the six subsidiary questions, and the message/thread schema. Implementation PR links to it.

2. **Thread model.** Chat threads persist under `.ddx/chat/<thread-id>/{manifest.json,messages.jsonl}`. One thread per project per operator by default; operators can create named threads. Messages.jsonl is append-only, one JSON object per message (user or assistant), with `sessionId` linking each assistant turn back to an entry in the sessions index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`).

3. **GraphQL surface.** New resolvers:
   - Query: `chatThreads(projectId)`, `chatThread(id)`, `chatMessages(threadId, since?)`.
   - Mutation: `chatThreadCreate(projectId, title?)`, `chatSend(threadId, message, harness?, profile?)` → kicks off a session via `service.Execute`.
   - Subscription: `chatStream(threadId)` → emits assistant message chunks + tool call events (same event vocabulary as `workers.recentEvents`).

4. **Home page UI.** Project home page gains a chat pane below the actions section. Pane shows thread list, active thread messages, input box, harness/profile picker. Tool calls render inline within assistant messages. Streaming updates as chunks arrive.

5. **Session linkage.** Each assistant turn creates one session in the sessions index. Clicking an assistant message shows the session id and a link to the session detail (`/sessions/<id>`). Chat thread header shows cumulative session count + total cost for the thread.

6. **Concurrency.** Two browsers open on the same thread see each other's messages within 2s (subscription-driven). Two operators on different threads in the same project do not interfere.

7. **Interrupt.** The in-flight assistant turn can be cancelled via a Stop button; server sends the harness the equivalent of `ddx agent workers stop <id>` for the in-flight session. The thread records a cancelled turn, does not try to auto-resume.

8. **Cost guardrail (soft).** If the cumulative thread cost passes a configurable ceiling (`.ddx/config.yaml: chat.cost_ceiling_usd`, default unset), the UI warns but does not block. Hard block is a follow-up.

9. **Auth posture.**
   - Per design note. If the chosen posture is "single-operator localhost / tsnet only for v1," the chat routes are served unauthenticated behind tsnet and the design note records the gap to close before broader deployment.
   - A small middleware gate is in place even for v1 so adding auth later is schema-local, not route-local.

10. **Playwright e2e.**
    - Seeds a project with zero chat threads.
    - Navigates to project home, asserts the chat pane renders with "Start a new thread" affordance.
    - Starts a thread, sends a short prompt, simulates a streamed session response with one tool call, asserts chunks appear in order and the session id link is present.
    - Opens a second browser context on the same thread, asserts both see new messages within 2s.
    - Cancels an in-flight turn, asserts the cancelled state is persisted.

11. **Depends on.**
    - `ddx-2513f08c` (home page reachable from sidebar) — hard dependency.
    - `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions index) — hard dependency for the session linkage + cost rollup.

12. **Explicitly deferred.** PTY integration (Option B / pop-out terminal from Option C) is its own bead, filed only if the Option A rollout surfaces real interactive-steering needs.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","closing_commit_sha":"9c29b5fe9cb5e06c3a0f1ca3d8133f8766b59259","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:51:38.374211227Z","description":"## Observed / Motivation

User wants to "lift work out of the TUI entirely and allow non-tmux users to work remotely." Today the only way to prompt the DDx agent stack interactively is a terminal session. The web UI is read-mostly: dispatch three actions (drain/align/checks) and review results. There is no surface that lets an operator say "go do X" against the project in natural language from a browser.

Related UX: the project home page already exists (`/nodes/.../projects/<id>/`), renders queue summary + actions, and is the natural host for a chat affordance. First the home page needs to be reachable from the sidebar (`ddx-2513f08c`) — this bead depends on that work.

## The tension

The DDx harness abstraction (`cli/internal/agent/service_run.go`, `service.Execute`) is designed for **non-interactive** use: prompt in, structured result out, optional streaming. This is the right shape for queue-draining, quorum, evaluation, and automation. It is **not** the shape of a TUI conversation with tab completion, mid-run steering, and back-channel signals.

An operator-facing chat can land on either side of that line. The bead records all four options so the design phase can pick one deliberately.

## Options (design phase decides, recommended path noted)

### Option A — Chat over the harness abstraction (RECOMMENDED starting point)

- Chat UI posts a prompt → server dispatches a session via `service.Execute` (or a wrapper) → streams tool calls + response chunks back over a GraphQL subscription.
- Message history is persisted as a thread; each user turn = one new session. Responses reuse the existing `recentEvents` stream shape already used on the workers detail page.
- Works with every harness today because the harness abstraction is what it is. claude, codex, agent (embedded) all participate.
- **Trade-off:** no mid-run steering, no tab completion, no live back-and-forth inside one agent invocation.
- Matches the user's stated target audience (non-TUI users who want remote browser access). Those users aren't trying to steer a TUI — they're trying to leave it behind.

### Option B — PTY gateway per harness

- Server owns a PTY pool. Each chat thread attaches to one harness process (claude/codex/etc.) over a WebSocket; browser renders xterm.js.
- Full interactivity, including TUI steering.
- **Trade-off:** PTY lifecycle, auth, resource limits, tab-close-loses-session semantics, multi-user access control — all real. The harness abstraction is bypassed for this mode, which creates a second execution path to maintain.

### Option C — Hybrid (chat-first + pop-out terminal)

- Option A is the primary surface. When a chat reaches a point that needs interactive steering, a "Open terminal" escape hatch attaches a PTY to the current harness session.
- **Trade-off:** more surface area, but honest about both use cases. Most sessions stay on the cheap path.

### Option D — Web-tmux / ttyd wrapper

- Embed the existing terminal tooling directly: ttyd or xterm.js over a tmux socket owned by the server.
- **Trade-off:** fastest to ship, zero harness work, but the UX is a terminal in a browser — not "chat." Requires tmux. Doesn't serve the stated "non-tmux users" audience.

### Recommendation

Start with **A**. If the design note documents real interactive-steering needs we can't paper over, the right follow-up is **C** (A + PTY escape hatch), not B (PTY-only).

## Scope of this bead

- Design note in `docs/helix/02-design/` locking the option, the message schema, persistence model, and auth posture. Implementation PR links to it.
- First implementation pass: Option A.
- Home page gains a chat pane as its primary interaction surface, alongside the existing queue summary + actions (which stay — they're quick-dispatch affordances, not replaced by chat).

## Subsidiary questions the design note must answer

1. **Message persistence.** Are chat threads per-project or per-user? Stored where (`.ddx/chat/<thread-id>/` or elsewhere)? Is history shared across operators on the same project, or isolated per operator identity?
2. **Harness selection.** Does the chat pick a harness per-thread (picker in the UI) or per-message (re-route each turn)? Default to profile-based selection?
3. **Cost accountability.** Every chat message spawns a session. Sessions are already tracked in the sessions index (`ddx-2ceb02fa`). The chat thread should surface cumulative cost to the operator so they don't blow budget unknowingly.
4. **Tool execution scope.** Chat prompts will trigger agent tool calls that read/write the project tree. Is the chat session bounded to the same permissions as `ddx agent run`? Does it run in the project worktree, a worktree per thread, a scratch dir? Default safe answer: same worktree as `ddx agent run` (user trusts ddx running anyway), but design note records it.
5. **Concurrency.** What happens if two operators have the chat open on the same project and both send messages? Default: independent threads, each with its own session — no shared state.
6. **Auth.** Today the server assumes single-operator localhost. Remote use implies some identity concept. Design note picks a posture: delegate to reverse proxy, ship a simple token, or punt and require tsnet + single-operator for v1.

## Out of scope

- Full PTY implementation (Option B). Follow-up bead if A+hybrid doesn't suffice.
- Cross-project chat.

Disposition: no commit this pass; recommend decomposing into 4–5 smaller beads.

(a) What is done in this pass
- Read the bead description and 12 acceptance criteria.
- Mapped the existing surface area:
  - GraphQL schema at cli/internal/server/graphql/schema.graphql with gqlgen-generated resolvers; subscription pattern established in resolver_sub_worker.go / resolver_sub_bead.go / resolver_sub_exec.go (buffered channel + ctx-cancel goroutine).
  - Frontend GraphQL via graphql-request (queries) + graphql-ws (subscriptions) — see cli/internal/server/frontend/src/lib/gql/{client.ts,subscriptions.ts}. No Houdini.
  - Project home page at cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/+page.svelte (queue summary + 3 action cards). This is the host surface for the chat pane.
  - Sessions index at cli/internal/agent/session_index.go, sharded JSONL under .ddx/sessions/ (monthly). agentSessions GraphQL query already exists. This is the linkage target for AC5.
  - Agent execution entry at cli/internal/agent/service_run.go (RunWithConfigViaService → executeOnService → records a session-index row). Streaming today is per-harness; the execute-loop streams events via worker progress, not per-prompt chunks.
  - Playwright e2e pattern in cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/**/*.e2e.ts uses page.route('/graphql') interception with fixture data; no live backend.

(b) What is blocking a one-pass implementation
The bead bundles a feature that fans out across at least 5 independently-reviewable surfaces:

  1. Storage layer (.ddx/chat/<thread-id>/{manifest.json,messages.jsonl}) — new Go package with tests, append-only invariants, project/operator scoping.
  2. GraphQL schema + resolvers — chatThreads/chatThread/chatMessages queries, chatThreadCreate/chatSend mutations, chatStream subscription. Requires schema.graphql edits, gqlgen regeneration of generated.go, models.go updates, four new resolver files, integration tests.
  3. service.Execute integration — chatSend must dispatch a session and stream chunks + tool calls back over the chatStream subscription. Today service.Execute is request/response with a non-streaming Result; per-chunk streaming over the subscription bus is new infrastructure. Either: (i) add a streaming variant of executeOnService, or (ii) tail the session's evidence stream and re-emit. Both have non-trivial design choices that belong in the design note before code lands.
  4. Frontend — chat pane component, thread list, harness/profile picker, streaming render, tool-call inline rendering, Stop button (AC7), cumulative cost header (AC5/AC8). Real-time updates via subscribeChatMessages helper. Roughly parallel in size to the workers detail page.
  5. Auth posture middleware (AC9) — even as a "single-operator localhost / tsnet for v1" stance, the design note must commit to a posture and a route-gate stub must exist so future auth is schema-local. The bead description flags this as load-bearing for the "remote ops" framing.
  6. Design note in docs/helix/02-design/ answering the six subsidiary questions (persistence scope, harness selection, cost accountability, tool-execution scope, concurrency, auth). The bead's AC1 makes this a hard prerequisite — the implementation PR is supposed to *link* to a landed design note. The note must precede or accompany the implementation; it cannot be a postscript.
  7. Playwright e2e (AC10) — five-step scenario including two browser contexts on the same thread, cancel mid-flight, streamed chunk ordering. Mocking the GraphQL subscription side of this is non-trivial; the existing e2e suite mocks queries via page.route but does not exercise graphql-ws subscriptions.

A single execute-bead commit covering all of the above would either (i) be partial across most of those surfaces and red, or (ii) take many hours of focused work and produce a sprawling diff that the review gate cannot meaningfully verify in one pass. Both outcomes are worse than a clean decomposition.

Additional concrete unknowns the design note must resolve before code can land cleanly:
- Is the chatStream subscription chunk-level (assistant text deltas) or event-level (one event per tool call + one final assistant message)? This determines whether service.Execute needs a new streaming surface or can be wrapped post-hoc.
- For multi-operator on the same thread (AC6), is the second operator a read-only observer or an equal participant who can also send? The bead defaults to "two browsers on the same thread see each other within 2s" which implies multi-participant — but the cost-accountability and session-attribution model needs a per-message author identity that today's auth posture (single-operator) does not surface.
- Does chatSend run in the project worktree or a per-thread worktree? Bead suggests "same as ddx agent run" as the default, but if two threads chatSend concurrently they will race the worktree. This argues for per-thread worktree, which is a real cost (disk, fs setup) and belongs in the design note.

(c) What a follow-up attempt would need

Recommend splitting this bead into the following sub-beads, executed in order:

  Sub-bead 1: Design note (docs/helix/02-design/chat-interface.md)
    - Picks Option A vs B vs C vs D and locks it.
    - Answers the six subsidiary questions.
    - Specifies the message/thread schema (manifest.json shape, messages.jsonl entry shape).
    - Specifies whether chatStream is chunk-level or event-level.
    - Specifies worktree scoping per thread.
    - Specifies auth posture for v1 and the gap to broader deployment.
    Size: 1 pass, design only. No code.

  Sub-bead 2: Chat thread storage package (cli/internal/chat/)
    - New Go package: ChatThread, ChatMessage, AppendMessage, LoadThread, ListThreads.
    - Append-only messages.jsonl invariants, atomic manifest writes.
    - Unit tests including concurrent append safety.
    Size: 1 pass.

  Sub-bead 3: GraphQL surface (schema + resolvers + integration tests)
    - schema.graphql edits, gqlgen regen, four resolver files, integration tests.
    - chatSend dispatches via service.Execute and writes the assistant turn as a single message once the session completes (event-level streaming, simplest first cut). Chunk-level streaming is a follow-up.
    - chatStream subscription emits message-created events.
    - Session linkage via SessionIndexEntry.id reference on each assistant message.
    Size: 1 pass.

  Sub-bead 4: Frontend chat pane
    - New ChatPane.svelte component, thread list, message list, input box, harness/profile picker.
    - Mounted on project home page below the actions section.
    - subscribeChatMessages helper in src/lib/gql/subscriptions.ts.
    - Cost rollup in thread header. Stop button calls workerStop on the in-flight session's worker.
    Size: 1 pass.

  Sub-bead 5: Playwright e2e + auth posture middleware
    - Two-context test, cancel test, streaming chunk test (with mocked graphql-ws server).
    - Auth middleware gate stub on chat routes.
    Size: 1 pass.

The bead's AC11 (depends-on ddx-2513f08c, ddx-2ceb02fa) was not verified in this pass. Sub-bead 1 should re-verify those deps before locking the design.

Recommendation: re-queue this bead as a tracking parent and create the five sub-beads above. The current bead description is unusually thorough and most of its content can be lifted verbatim into the design-note sub-bead's body.

.ddx/beads.jsonl:757:{"acceptance":"1. `TestWorkLoop_ClaimLivenessDoesNotMutateBeadsJSONL` verifies a running claimed-bead liveness tick does not change `.ddx/beads.jsonl` bytes or the bead `updated_at` field.
2. `TestWorkLoop_LivenessRecordedOutsideTracker` verifies the replacement liveness surface is written/read outside `.ddx/beads.jsonl` and is discoverable by worker/watchdog code.
3. `TestStoreHeartbeat_RemovedOrNoTrackerWrite` verifies `Store.Heartbeat` is removed from the work-loop call path or becomes a non-mutating/deprecated no-op with no tracker write.
4. WIRED-IN: `git grep "Store.Heartbeat|.Heartbeat(candidate.ID)|.Heartbeat(beadID)" cli/internal/agent cli/internal/server` shows no periodic worker liveness path writing through the bead JSONL store.
5. C11 (`ddx-0b8780db`) either depends on this bead or its AC is updated to extract the safe liveness path, not the existing tracker-writing heartbeat.
6. cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/... ./internal/bead/... ./internal/server/... -run "Heartbeat|Liveness|Watchdog|Claim" -count=1 passes.

8. lefthook run pre-commit passes.","claimed-at":"2026-05-06T08:50:08Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"601864","created_at":"2026-05-06T05:05:19.168749536Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-9f20a4bd","depends_on_id":"ddx-06eb05d8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-06T05:05:24Z"}],"description":"PROBLEM
`ddx work` currently refreshes claimed-bead liveness by rewriting `.ddx/beads.jsonl` every heartbeat interval. In this repo that dirties the git worktree continuously, creates tracker-only commit noise, increases merge conflict risk on the hottest tracker file, and mixes ephemeral worker liveness with durable bead state. FEAT-006 also states the worker/server boundary has no claim lease or heartbeat, so durable tracker heartbeats conflict with the intended execution contract.

ROOT CAUSE WITH FILE:LINE
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go:817-832 starts a heartbeat goroutine for each claimed candidate and calls `w.Store.Heartbeat(beadID)` on every ticker interval.
- cli/internal/bead/store.go:666-687 implements `Store.Heartbeat` by writing `execute-loop-heartbeat-at` and `updated_at` back into `.ddx/beads.jsonl`.
- cli/internal/config/types.go:160-164 and 198-206 expose a 30s default worker heartbeat interval, making the tracker rewrite periodic during normal operation.

PROPOSED FIX
- Stop writing periodic liveness heartbeats to `.ddx/beads.jsonl`. Durable tracker mutations should remain limited to semantic transitions such as claim, unclaim, close, cooldown, and append-only evidence/events.
- Replace `Store.Heartbeat` usage in the work loop with an ephemeral liveness surface that does not dirty the tracker. Acceptable implementations include an ignored runtime lease file under `.ddx/workers/` or `.ddx/runs/`, server worker state, or another non-tracker runtime store.
- Preserve stale-claim/watchdog behavior by teaching any stale-worker logic to read the new ephemeral liveness surface or, if no reliable runtime heartbeat exists, to rely only on explicit claim metadata plus process/server lifecycle cleanup.
- Update C11/heartbeat extraction expectations if necessary so it extracts or removes the new safe liveness path rather than preserving tracker writes.

NON-SCOPE
- Changing bead claim semantics (`status=in_progress`, owner, claimed-at, claimed-pid).
- Removing append-only execution evidence or semantic bead events.
- Implementing the Axon bead backend; this bug applies to the JSONL tracker path.

DEPS
- Depends on ddx-06eb05d8 because C7 is currently changing the same drain-loop area.

INTERSECTIONS
- ddx-0b8780db (C11 heartbeat extraction) should depend on this bead so the extraction does not preserve tracker-writing heartbeat behavior.
- ddx-5cb6e6cd execution refactor should not close while periodic `.ddx/beads.jsonl` liveness writes remain in the work loop.","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-05-06T08:50:08.999109499Z","id":"ddx-9f20a4bd","issue_type":"bug","labels":["phase:2","area:agent","area:beads","kind:bug","reliability","refactor","spec:FEAT-006","spec:FEAT-010"],"owner":"erik","parent":"ddx-5cb6e6cd","priority":0,"spec-id":"FEAT-006","status":"in_progress","title":"agent: move claim heartbeat out of beads.jsonl","updated_at":"2026-05-06T08:50:08.999109499Z"}
.ddx/executions/20260506T005752-1f271178/manifest.json:12:    "description": "PROBLEM
  The drain loop tracks attempted and hookFailed beads via ad-hoc in-memory maps rather than a structured Guard contract, making the loop body untestable in isolation and the skip policy implicit.

ROOT CAUSE
  cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go:403-413 declares attempted := make(map[string]struct{}) and hookFailed := make(map[string]struct{}). Lines 534-544 manually clear both maps between sleep cycles. Lines 591-601 implement the two-strikes pre-claim hook policy by mutating these maps inline. nextCandidate (line 1216) accepts attempted as a parameter and checks membership at line 1223. No Guard interface exists; the pre-claim logic and the complexity gate each embed their skip logic directly in the loop body.

PROPOSED FIX
  - Define Guard interface in cli/internal/agent/work/guard.go: Allow(ctx context.Context, beadID string) (bool, reason string).
  - Implement two concrete Guards: PreClaimGuard (wraps runtime.PreClaimHook two-strikes logic) and ComplexityGuard (wraps w.ComplexityGate nil-check).
  - Delete attempted and hookFailed maps from execute_bead_loop.go:403-413. Replace nextCandidate's attempted parameter with drain.result.Results slice (already populated per-iteration): filter beads whose ID appears in Results.
  - Pre-claim hook denial: call w.Store.SetExecutionCooldown(candidate.ID, 30s, ...) instead of recording in hookFailed.
  - Add TestGuard_PreClaim_TwoStrikesSkips and TestGuard_Complexity_NilGateWarnsOnce to a new cli/internal/agent/work/guard_test.go.

NON-SCOPE
  - Heartbeat extraction (C11, ddx-0b8780db).
  - File renames (C13, ddx-387a0178).
  - Any change to SetExecutionCooldown duration policy (controlled by caller constants).

INTERSECTIONS
- P1: guard denials should skip the candidate rather than wedge the queue.
- P2: each guard owns only its own skip policy.
- P4: a denied bead stays isolated from other beads.",

.ddx/executions/20260505T103941-e21c5fa9/manifest.json:12:    "description": "PROBLEM
  The conflict-recovery state machine (auto-merge + focused-resolve + close-or-park) is partially extracted but the description cites only the pre-extraction loop lines, leaving the executor without a clear picture of the current API boundary.

ROOT CAUSE
  cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_conflict_recovery.go:118 defines RunConflictRecovery(ctx, ConflictRecoveryInput) ConflictRecoveryOutput — the C4 extraction already landed. The bead description still refers to the old inline branch. The caller site is cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go:847-878 (loop branches on ShouldAttemptConflictRecovery, then calls RunConflictRecovery). The try.Attempt API (cli/internal/agent/try/attempt.go:67, func Attempt(ctx, store, beadID, AttemptOpts) (Outcome, error)) does not yet call RunConflictRecovery; conflict recovery remains loop-owned.

PROPOSED FIX
  - Migrate RunConflictRecovery invocation from execute_bead_loop.go:847-878 into try.Attempt (cli/internal/agent/try/attempt.go) so Attempt's return Outcome already encodes conflict-recovery disposition.
  - Add try/conflict_recovery.go: thin adapter from ConflictRecoveryOutput to Outcome (Disposition=Merged → OutcomeSuccess, Park/NeedsHuman → OutcomePark).
  - Loop caller (execute_bead_loop.go:847) delegates to try.Attempt instead of calling RunConflictRecovery directly; removes the inline branch.
  - Add TestAttempt_ConflictRecovered_ReturnsMergedOutcome and TestAttempt_ConflictUnresolvable_ReturnsPark to cli/internal/agent/try/attempt_test.go.

NON-SCOPE
  - Deleting the attempted/hookFailed maps (C7, ddx-06eb05d8).
  - Heartbeat extraction (C11, ddx-0b8780db).
  - File renames (C13, ddx-387a0178).

INTERSECTIONS
- P1: fail-open at the layer boundary when conflict recovery is not applicable or cannot complete cleanly.
- P2: conflict recovery owns only its own conditions; the loop should delegate rather than infer recovery policy.
- P3: the worker panel should surface conflict-recovery transitions and any degraded path.
- P4: a bad bead X must not spill into bead Y; conflict recovery stays per-bead.",

.ddx/executions/20260428T223713-1faefc18/result.json:9:  "no_changes_rationale": "Bead: ddx-88e91fdb — Add chat interface to project home page

Disposition: no commit this pass; recommend decomposing into 4–5 smaller beads.

(a) What is done in this pass
- Read the bead description and 12 acceptance criteria.
- Mapped the existing surface area:
  - GraphQL schema at cli/internal/server/graphql/schema.graphql with gqlgen-generated resolvers; subscription pattern established in resolver_sub_worker.go / resolver_sub_bead.go / resolver_sub_exec.go (buffered channel + ctx-cancel goroutine).
  - Frontend GraphQL via graphql-request (queries) + graphql-ws (subscriptions) — see cli/internal/server/frontend/src/lib/gql/{client.ts,subscriptions.ts}. No Houdini.
  - Project home page at cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/+page.svelte (queue summary + 3 action cards). This is the host surface for the chat pane.
  - Sessions index at cli/internal/agent/session_index.go, sharded JSONL under .ddx/sessions/ (monthly). agentSessions GraphQL query already exists. This is the linkage target for AC5.
  - Agent execution entry at cli/internal/agent/service_run.go (RunWithConfigViaService → executeOnService → records a session-index row). Streaming today is per-harness; the execute-loop streams events via worker progress, not per-prompt chunks.
  - Playwright e2e pattern in cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/**/*.e2e.ts uses page.route('/graphql') interception with fixture data; no live backend.

(b) What is blocking a one-pass implementation
The bead bundles a feature that fans out across at least 5 independently-reviewable surfaces:

  1. Storage layer (.ddx/chat/<thread-id>/{manifest.json,messages.jsonl}) — new Go package with tests, append-only invariants, project/operator scoping.
  2. GraphQL schema + resolvers — chatThreads/chatThread/chatMessages queries, chatThreadCreate/chatSend mutations, chatStream subscription. Requires schema.graphql edits, gqlgen regeneration of generated.go, models.go updates, four new resolver files, integration tests.
  3. service.Execute integration — chatSend must dispatch a session and stream chunks + tool calls back over the chatStream subscription. Today service.Execute is request/response with a non-streaming Result; per-chunk streaming over the subscription bus is new infrastructure. Either: (i) add a streaming variant of executeOnService, or (ii) tail the session's evidence stream and re-emit. Both have non-trivial design choices that belong in the design note before code lands.
  4. Frontend — chat pane component, thread list, harness/profile picker, streaming render, tool-call inline rendering, Stop button (AC7), cumulative cost header (AC5/AC8). Real-time updates via subscribeChatMessages helper. Roughly parallel in size to the workers detail page.
  5. Auth posture middleware (AC9) — even as a "single-operator localhost / tsnet for v1" stance, the design note must commit to a posture and a route-gate stub must exist so future auth is schema-local. The bead description flags this as load-bearing for the "remote ops" framing.
  6. Design note in docs/helix/02-design/ answering the six subsidiary questions (persistence scope, harness selection, cost accountability, tool-execution scope, concurrency, auth). The bead's AC1 makes this a hard prerequisite — the implementation PR is supposed to *link* to a landed design note. The note must precede or accompany the implementation; it cannot be a postscript.
  7. Playwright e2e (AC10) — five-step scenario including two browser contexts on the same thread, cancel mid-flight, streamed chunk ordering. Mocking the GraphQL subscription side of this is non-trivial; the existing e2e suite mocks queries via page.route but does not exercise graphql-ws subscriptions.

A single execute-bead commit covering all of the above would either (i) be partial across most of those surfaces and red, or (ii) take many hours of focused work and produce a sprawling diff that the review gate cannot meaningfully verify in one pass. Both outcomes are worse than a clean decomposition.

Additional concrete unknowns the design note must resolve before code can land cleanly:
- Is the chatStream subscription chunk-level (assistant text deltas) or event-level (one event per tool call + one final assistant message)? This determines whether service.Execute needs a new streaming surface or can be wrapped post-hoc.
- For multi-operator on the same thread (AC6), is the second operator a read-only observer or an equal participant who can also send? The bead defaults to "two browsers on the same thread see each other within 2s" which implies multi-participant — but the cost-accountability and session-attribution model needs a per-message author identity that today's auth posture (single-operator) does not surface.
- Does chatSend run in the project worktree or a per-thread worktree? Bead suggests "same as ddx agent run" as the default, but if two threads chatSend concurrently they will race the worktree. This argues for per-thread worktree, which is a real cost (disk, fs setup) and belongs in the design note.

(c) What a follow-up attempt would need

Recommend splitting this bead into the following sub-beads, executed in order:

  Sub-bead 1: Design note (docs/helix/02-design/chat-interface.md)
    - Picks Option A vs B vs C vs D and locks it.
    - Answers the six subsidiary questions.
    - Specifies the message/thread schema (manifest.json shape, messages.jsonl entry shape).
    - Specifies whether chatStream is chunk-level or event-level.
    - Specifies worktree scoping per thread.
    - Specifies auth posture for v1 and the gap to broader deployment.
    Size: 1 pass, design only. No code.

  Sub-bead 2: Chat thread storage package (cli/internal/chat/)
    - New Go package: ChatThread, ChatMessage, AppendMessage, LoadThread, ListThreads.
    - Append-only messages.jsonl invariants, atomic manifest writes.
    - Unit tests including concurrent append safety.
    Size: 1 pass.

  Sub-bead 3: GraphQL surface (schema + resolvers + integration tests)
    - schema.graphql edits, gqlgen regen, four resolver files, integration tests.
    - chatSend dispatches via service.Execute and writes the assistant turn as a single message once the session completes (event-level streaming, simplest first cut). Chunk-level streaming is a follow-up.
    - chatStream subscription emits message-created events.
    - Session linkage via SessionIndexEntry.id reference on each assistant message.
    Size: 1 pass.

  Sub-bead 4: Frontend chat pane
    - New ChatPane.svelte component, thread list, message list, input box, harness/profile picker.
    - Mounted on project home page below the actions section.
    - subscribeChatMessages helper in src/lib/gql/subscriptions.ts.
    - Cost rollup in thread header. Stop button calls workerStop on the in-flight session's worker.
    Size: 1 pass.

  Sub-bead 5: Playwright e2e + auth posture middleware
    - Two-context test, cancel test, streaming chunk test (with mocked graphql-ws server).
    - Auth middleware gate stub on chat routes.
    Size: 1 pass.

The bead's AC11 (depends-on ddx-2513f08c, ddx-2ceb02fa) was not verified in this pass. Sub-bead 1 should re-verify those deps before locking the design.

Recommendation: re-queue this bead as a tracking parent and create the five sub-beads above. The current bead description is unusually thorough and most of its content can be lifted verbatim into the design-note sub-bead's body.",
